title: Cobb-Douglas Marshallian Demand and Indirect Utility
learning_objective: Derive Marshallian demand and indirect utility for an interior smooth consumer problem.
preliminaries: Let prices satisfy \(p_1,p_2>0\) and wealth \(w>0\). A Marshallian demand solves \(\max_{x_1,x_2\ge 0} u(x_1,x_2)\) subject to \(p_1x_1+p_2x_2\le w\).
question: A consumer has utility \(u(x_1,x_2)=x_1^{\alpha}x_2^{1-\alpha}\), where \(0<\alpha<1\). Derive the Marshallian demand \(x(p,w)\), verify Walras' law, and compute the indirect utility function \(v(p,w)\).
solution: Because utility is strictly increasing, the budget constraint binds. For an interior optimum, maximize \(\alpha\ln x_1+(1-\alpha)\ln x_2\) subject to the budget. The first-order conditions are \(\alpha/x_1=\lambda p_1\) and \((1-\alpha)/x_2=\lambda p_2\). Hence \(p_1x_1=\alpha/\lambda\) and \(p_2x_2=(1-\alpha)/\lambda\). Adding and using the binding budget gives \(1/\lambda=w\). Therefore \(x_1(p,w)=\alpha w/p_1\) and \(x_2(p,w)=(1-\alpha)w/p_2\). Walras' law holds since \(p_1x_1+p_2x_2=\alpha w+(1-\alpha)w=w\). Substitution into utility yields \(v(p,w)=(\alpha w/p_1)^{\alpha}((1-\alpha)w/p_2)^{1-\alpha}=w\alpha^{\alpha}(1-\alpha)^{1-\alpha}/(p_1^{\alpha}p_2^{1-\alpha})\). Strict concavity of \(\ln u\) gives uniqueness.

title: Cobb-Douglas Elasticities and Homogeneity
learning_objective: Compute price and wealth elasticities and verify homogeneity restrictions implied by demand theory.
preliminaries: For a differentiable demand \(x_i(p,w)\), the own-price elasticity is \(\varepsilon_{ii}=\partial x_i/\partial p_i\cdot p_i/x_i\), and the wealth elasticity is \(\eta_i=\partial x_i/\partial w\cdot w/x_i\).
question: Use the Cobb-Douglas demand from the canonical problem, \(x_1=\alpha w/p_1\) and \(x_2=(1-\alpha)w/p_2\). Compute all own-price, cross-price, and wealth elasticities. Then verify that the demand is homogeneous of degree zero in \((p,w)\).
solution: For good 1, \(\partial x_1/\partial p_1=-\alpha w/p_1^2\), so \(\varepsilon_{11}=(-\alpha w/p_1^2)(p_1/(\alpha w/p_1))=-1\). Since \(x_1\) does not depend on \(p_2\), \(\varepsilon_{12}=0\). Also \(\partial x_1/\partial w=\alpha/p_1\), so \(\eta_1=(\alpha/p_1)(w/(\alpha w/p_1))=1\). Similarly, \(\varepsilon_{22}=-1\), \(\varepsilon_{21}=0\), and \(\eta_2=1\). For any \(t>0\), \(x_1(tp,tw)=\alpha tw/(tp_1)=x_1(p,w)\) and \(x_2(tp,tw)=(1-\alpha)tw/(tp_2)=x_2(p,w)\). Thus demand is homogeneous of degree zero. The result also illustrates that Cobb-Douglas budget shares are constant and wealth elasticities are one.

title: CES Demand with Two Goods
learning_objective: Derive Marshallian demand for a constant elasticity of substitution utility function.
preliminaries: Let \(p_1,p_2,w>0\). For \(0<\rho<1\), define \(u(x_1,x_2)=(a x_1^{\rho}+(1-a)x_2^{\rho})^{1/\rho}\), where \(0<a<1\). Since the outer power is increasing, the same bundles maximize \(a x_1^{\rho}+(1-a)x_2^{\rho}\).
question: Derive the Marshallian demand for the CES utility in the preliminaries. Express demand in terms of prices, wealth, \(a\), and \(\rho\).
solution: The objective is strictly concave for \(0<\rho<1\), so the interior first-order conditions are sufficient. They are \(a\rho x_1^{\rho-1}=\lambda p_1\) and \((1-a)\rho x_2^{\rho-1}=\lambda p_2\). Dividing gives \((x_1/x_2)^{\rho-1}=((1-a)p_1)/(a p_2)\). Let \(\sigma=1/(1-\rho)>1\). Then \(x_1/x_2=(a p_2/((1-a)p_1))^{\sigma}\). Write \(r=(a p_2/((1-a)p_1))^{\sigma}\), so \(x_1=rx_2\). The binding budget implies \((p_1r+p_2)x_2=w\), hence \(x_2=w/(p_1r+p_2)\) and \(x_1=rw/(p_1r+p_2)\). Equivalently, after multiplying numerator and denominator by \(((1-a)p_1)^{\sigma}\), \(x_1=w a^{\sigma}p_1^{-\sigma}/(a^{\sigma}p_1^{1-\sigma}+(1-a)^{\sigma}p_2^{1-\sigma})\), and \(x_2=w(1-a)^{\sigma}p_2^{-\sigma}/(a^{\sigma}p_1^{1-\sigma}+(1-a)^{\sigma}p_2^{1-\sigma})\).

title: Quasilinear Demand with a Boundary Case
learning_objective: Handle interior and boundary cases in a quasilinear utility maximization problem.
preliminaries: Let \(x\ge 0\) be consumption of a nonlinear good with price \(p>0\), and let \(m\ge 0\) be numeraire consumption with price one. Wealth is \(W>0\).
question: A consumer maximizes \(u(x,m)=2\sqrt{x}+m\) subject to \(px+m\le W\), \(x,m\ge 0\). Derive demand for \(x\) and \(m\) for all \(p,W>0\).
solution: Utility is increasing, so \(px+m=W\). Substitute \(m=W-px\), with \(0\le x\le W/p\), and maximize \(2\sqrt{x}+W-px\). For \(x>0\), the derivative is \(1/\sqrt{x}-p\). The unconstrained optimum is \(x=1/p^2\). This is feasible if \(1/p^2\le W/p\), equivalently \(W\ge 1/p\). In that case \(x^*=1/p^2\) and \(m^*=W-1/p\). If \(W<1/p\), the derivative at all feasible \(x<W/p\) is positive at the upper endpoint condition, so the consumer spends all wealth on \(x\): \(x^*=W/p\) and \(m^*=0\). Thus \(x^*(p,W)=\min\{1/p^2,W/p\}\) and \(m^*(p,W)=W-px^*(p,W)\). The threshold is where the desired quasilinear demand just exhausts wealth.

title: Perfect Complements Demand and Indirect Utility
learning_objective: Solve a nonsmooth consumer problem by using the structure of perfect complements.
preliminaries: Let \(p_1,p_2,w>0\). A consumer has preferences represented by \(u(x_1,x_2)=\min\{x_1,2x_2\}\) over \(\mathbb{R}_+^2\).
question: Find the Marshallian demand and indirect utility. Explain why the optimal bundle lies on the kink unless wealth is zero.
solution: If \(x_1>2x_2\), reducing \(x_1\) while holding \(x_2\) fixed lowers expenditure without lowering utility, so such a bundle cannot be cost-effective at an optimum with positive wealth. If \(x_1<2x_2\), reducing \(x_2\) similarly saves money without lowering utility. Hence any positive optimum has \(x_1=2x_2\), and the budget binds. Substitute into \(p_1x_1+p_2x_2=w\): \(p_1(2x_2)+p_2x_2=w\), so \(x_2=w/(2p_1+p_2)\) and \(x_1=2w/(2p_1+p_2)\). The utility attained is \(u=x_1=2x_2=2w/(2p_1+p_2)\). Thus \(v(p,w)=2w/(2p_1+p_2)\). The argument does not rely on differentiability; it uses the fact that excess of either component beyond the fixed ratio is wasted.

title: Testing WARP with Two Observations
learning_objective: Use price and quantity observations to test the weak axiom of revealed preference.
preliminaries: Observation \(t\) consists of prices \(p^t\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^L\), chosen bundle \(x^t\in\mathbb{R}_+^L\), and wealth \(w^t=p^t\cdot x^t\). Bundle \(x^t\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^s\) if \(p^t\cdot x^t\ge p^t\cdot x^s\). WARP is violated if \(x^t\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^s\), \(x^s\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^t\), and the two bundles differ, with at least one strict affordability comparison.
question: A consumer has two observations: \(p^1=(1,2)\), \(x^1=(4,1)\), and \(p^2=(2,1)\), \(x^2=(1,4)\). Test WARP and state the revealed preference relations.
solution: Compute expenditures. At prices \(p^1\), the chosen bundle costs \(p^1\cdot x^1=1\cdot4+2\cdot1=6\). The other bundle costs \(p^1\cdot x^2=1\cdot1+2\cdot4=9\). Since \(x^2\) was not affordable in observation 1, \(x^1\) is not directly revealed preferred to \(x^2\). At prices \(p^2\), the chosen bundle costs \(p^2\cdot x^2=2\cdot1+1\cdot4=6\). The other bundle costs \(p^2\cdot x^1=2\cdot4+1\cdot1=9\). Thus \(x^1\) was not affordable in observation 2, so \(x^2\) is not directly revealed preferred to \(x^1\). There is no revealed preference relation between the two observations and hence no WARP violation.

title: A Numeric WARP Nonviolation
learning_objective: Distinguish a WARP violation from one-way revealed preference using expenditure inequalities.
preliminaries: Use the WARP definitions from the canonical WARP problem. A strict WARP violation occurs when each chosen bundle was affordable when the other was chosen and the bundles differ.
question: A consumer has observations \(p^1=(1,1)\), \(x^1=(2,2)\), and \(p^2=(2,1)\), \(x^2=(1,1)\). Does the data violate WARP?
solution: At observation 1, wealth is \(p^1\cdot x^1=4\). The second bundle would have cost \(p^1\cdot x^2=2\), so \(x^2\) was affordable when \(x^1\) was chosen. Therefore \(x^1\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^2\). At observation 2, wealth is \(p^2\cdot x^2=3\). The first bundle would have cost \(p^2\cdot x^1=2\cdot2+1\cdot2=6\), so \(x^1\) was not affordable when \(x^2\) was chosen. Hence \(x^2\) is not directly revealed preferred to \(x^1\). The data do not violate WARP. A common error is to compare coordinatewise bundle sizes; WARP depends on affordability at the observed prices, not on whether one bundle contains more of every good.

source.family: Mas-Colell, Whinston, and Green Microeconomic Theory

title: Constructing a Strict WARP Violation
learning_objective: Construct and verify a two-observation dataset that violates WARP.
preliminaries: For observations \((p^t,x^t)\), let wealth equal observed expenditure \(p^t\cdot x^t\). WARP forbids choosing \(x^t\) over an affordable distinct \(x^s\) and also choosing \(x^s\) over an affordable distinct \(x^t\).
question: Consider \(p^1=(1,1)\), \(x^1=(2,0)\), \(p^2=(1,1)\), and \(x^2=(0,2)\). Show that these observations violate WARP. Then explain why the violation is not removed by the fact that prices are identical.
solution: At prices \(p^1=(1,1)\), observed wealth is \(2\), and \(p^1\cdot x^2=2\). Thus \(x^2\) was affordable when \(x^1\) was chosen, so \(x^1\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^2\). At prices \(p^2=(1,1)\), observed wealth is also \(2\), and \(p^2\cdot x^1=2\). Thus \(x^1\) was affordable when \(x^2\) was chosen, so \(x^2\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^1\). The bundles are distinct. Therefore WARP is violated: the same budget set generated two different single-valued choices. Identical prices make the violation especially stark; if the demand function is single-valued, one budget cannot rationalize two different chosen bundles.

source.locator: revealed preference and GARP discussion around lines 4292-4457

title: A Three-Observation GARP Cycle
learning_objective: Detect a generalized axiom of revealed preference violation through a revealed preference chain.
preliminaries: Bundle \(x^t\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^s\) if \(p^t\cdot x^t\ge p^t\cdot x^s\), and strictly directly revealed preferred if the inequality is strict. GARP rules out a chain \(x^1 R x^2 R \cdots R x^n\) together with \(x^n\) strictly directly revealed preferred to \(x^1\).
question: Consider three observations: \(p^1=(1,0.4)\), \(x^1=(1,0)\); \(p^2=(0.4,1)\), \(x^2=(0,1)\); and \(p^3=(1,1)\), \(x^3=(0.6,0.6)\). Test GARP.
solution: At observation 1, wealth is \(1\). The cost of \(x^3\) is \(1\cdot0.6+0.4\cdot0.6=0.84\), so \(x^1\) is strictly directly revealed preferred to \(x^3\). At observation 3, wealth is \(1.2\). The cost of \(x^2\) is \(1\), so \(x^3\) is strictly directly revealed preferred to \(x^2\). At observation 2, wealth is \(1\). The cost of \(x^1\) is \(0.4\), so \(x^2\) is strictly directly revealed preferred to \(x^1\). Thus there is a strict revealed preference cycle \(x^1 R x^3 R x^2 R x^1\). This violates GARP and hence cannot be rationalized by a locally nonsatiated utility function that chooses these bundles from the corresponding budgets.

title: Laspeyres and Paasche Quantity Indexes
learning_objective: Interpret quantity indexes using revealed preference inequalities.
preliminaries: For periods 0 and 1, let \(p^t\) be prices and \(x^t\) chosen quantities. The Laspeyres quantity index is \(L_q=p^0\cdot x^1/(p^0\cdot x^0)\), and the Paasche quantity index is \(P_q=p^1\cdot x^1/(p^1\cdot x^0)\).
question: Prove: if \(L_q<1\), then \(x^0\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^1\). If \(P_q>1\), then \(x^1\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^0\).
solution: If \(L_q<1\), then \(p^0\cdot x^1<p^0\cdot x^0\). At period 0 prices, bundle \(x^1\) cost strictly less than the chosen bundle \(x^0\), so \(x^1\) was affordable when \(x^0\) was chosen. Therefore \(x^0\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^1\), strictly if the definition records strict affordability. If \(P_q>1\), then \(p^1\cdot x^1>p^1\cdot x^0\). At period 1 prices, bundle \(x^0\) was affordable when \(x^1\) was chosen. Hence \(x^1\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^0\). These conclusions use only observed choices and budget inequalities; they do not require a utility representation or price index theory beyond the definitions.

source.family: Mas-Colell, Whinston, and Green Microeconomic Theory

title: WARP Implies the Compensated Law of Demand
learning_objective: Prove the compensated law of demand from revealed preference reasoning.
preliminaries: Let \(x(p,w)\) be a single-valued demand satisfying Walras' law. Starting from \((p,w)\) with \(x=x(p,w)\), define a Slutsky-compensated change to \(p'\) by setting \(w'=p'\cdot x\), and let \(x'=x(p',w')\).
question: Assume demand satisfies WARP and Walras' law. Prove that \((p'-p)\cdot(x'-x)\le 0\) for every Slutsky-compensated price change.
solution: By construction, \(p'\cdot x=w'\), so the old bundle \(x\) is affordable at the new budget. Since \(x'\) is chosen at \((p',w')\), \(x'\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x\). Suppose, toward contradiction, that \((p'-p)\cdot(x'-x)>0\). Since Walras' law gives \(p'\cdot x'=w'=p'\cdot x\), we have \(p'\cdot(x'-x)=0\). The strict inequality then implies \(-p\cdot(x'-x)>0\), or \(p\cdot x'<p\cdot x=w\). Thus \(x'\) was strictly affordable at the original budget when \(x\) was chosen, so \(x\) is strictly directly revealed preferred to \(x'\). We now have \(x'\) revealed preferred to \(x\) and \(x\) strictly revealed preferred to \(x'\), contradicting WARP. Therefore \((p'-p)\cdot(x'-x)\le 0\).

source.family: Mas-Colell, Whinston, and Green Microeconomic Theory
source.locator: Chapter 2 Section 2.F summary and exercise 2.F.13 on WARP and compensated law of demand

title: Cobb-Douglas Expenditure and Hicksian Demand
learning_objective: Derive an expenditure function and Hicksian demand, and verify Shephard's lemma.
preliminaries: For prices \(p_1,p_2>0\) and target utility \(u>0\), the expenditure function is \(e(p,u)=\min_{x\ge 0} p_1x_1+p_2x_2\) subject to \(x_1^{\alpha}x_2^{1-\alpha}\ge u\), where \(0<\alpha<1\).
question: Derive \(e(p,u)\) and Hicksian demand \(h(p,u)\). Verify that \(\partial e/\partial p_i=h_i\).
solution: At the optimum the utility constraint binds and the solution is interior. The first-order conditions for minimizing cost are \(p_1=\lambda\alpha x_1^{\alpha-1}x_2^{1-\alpha}\) and \(p_2=\lambda(1-\alpha)x_1^{\alpha}x_2^{-\alpha}\). Dividing gives \(p_1/p_2=(\alpha/(1-\alpha))(x_2/x_1)\), so cost shares satisfy \(p_1x_1=\alpha C\) and \(p_2x_2=(1-\alpha)C\), where \(C=e(p,u)\). Hence \(x_1=\alpha C/p_1\) and \(x_2=(1-\alpha)C/p_2\). Plugging into utility gives \(u=(\alpha C/p_1)^{\alpha}((1-\alpha)C/p_2)^{1-\alpha}=C\alpha^{\alpha}(1-\alpha)^{1-\alpha}/(p_1^{\alpha}p_2^{1-\alpha})\). Thus \(e(p,u)=u p_1^{\alpha}p_2^{1-\alpha}/(\alpha^{\alpha}(1-\alpha)^{1-\alpha})\). Hicksian demands are \(h_1=\alpha e/p_1\) and \(h_2=(1-\alpha)e/p_2\). Differentiating \(e\) with respect to \(p_1\) and \(p_2\) gives exactly these expressions, verifying Shephard's lemma.

title: Roy's Identity for an Indirect Utility Function
learning_objective: Recover Marshallian demand from an indirect utility function using Roy's identity.
preliminaries: Roy's identity states that if indirect utility \(v(p,w)\) is differentiable and locally nonsatiated demand is single-valued, then \(x_i(p,w)=-v_{p_i}(p,w)/v_w(p,w)\).
question: Let \(v(p_1,p_2,w)=w/(p_1^{1/3}p_2^{2/3})\). Use Roy's identity to recover Marshallian demand and verify Walras' law.
solution: Compute derivatives: \(v_w=1/(p_1^{1/3}p_2^{2/3})\), \(v_{p_1}=-(1/3)w p_1^{-4/3}p_2^{-2/3}\), and \(v_{p_2}=-(2/3)w p_1^{-1/3}p_2^{-5/3}\). Roy's identity gives \(x_1=-v_{p_1}/v_w=(1/3)w/p_1\) and \(x_2=-v_{p_2}/v_w=(2/3)w/p_2\). Walras' law holds because \(p_1x_1+p_2x_2=w/3+2w/3=w\). The recovered demand corresponds to Cobb-Douglas budget shares \((1/3,2/3)\), which is consistent with the homogeneity of \(v\) of degree zero in \((p,w)\).

title: Equivalent and Compensating Variation from Expenditure
learning_objective: Compute welfare measures for a price change using the expenditure function.
preliminaries: For an initial price \(p^0\), new price \(p^1\), and wealth \(w\), let \(u^0=v(p^0,w)\) and \(u^1=v(p^1,w)\). Compensating variation for a price increase is \(CV=e(p^1,u^0)-w\). Equivalent variation is \(EV=w-e(p^0,u^1)\).
question: A consumer has \(u(x_1,x_2)=\sqrt{x_1x_2}\), initial prices \(p^0=(1,1)\), new prices \(p^1=(4,1)\), and wealth \(w=100\). Compute \(CV\) and \(EV\).
solution: For \(u=\sqrt{x_1x_2}=x_1^{1/2}x_2^{1/2}\), the expenditure function is \(e(p,u)=2u\sqrt{p_1p_2}\). At initial prices, indirect utility is \(u^0=w/(2\sqrt{p_1p_2})=100/2=50\). At new prices, \(u^1=100/(2\sqrt{4})=25\). Compensating variation is the extra wealth at new prices needed to reach old utility: \(CV=e(p^1,u^0)-100=2\cdot50\cdot\sqrt{4}-100=200-100=100\). Equivalent variation is the wealth at old prices the consumer would give up to be as well off as after the price increase: \(EV=100-e(p^0,u^1)=100-2\cdot25\cdot1=50\). The price increase hurts the consumer; both numbers are positive losses, with \(CV>EV\) for this case.

title: Slutsky Symmetry as an Integrability Test
learning_objective: Use the Slutsky matrix to reject a candidate demand system as utility-generated.
preliminaries: For two goods, define the Slutsky terms \(S_{ij}=\partial x_i/\partial p_j+x_j\partial x_i/\partial w\). A differentiable demand generated by smooth preferences must have a symmetric Slutsky matrix where interior regularity conditions apply.
question: Consider the demand system \(x_1(p,w)=w/(2p_1)+p_2\) and \(x_2(p,w)=w/(2p_2)-p_1p_2/p_2\), on a domain where both quantities are positive. Does it pass Slutsky symmetry?
solution: First simplify \(x_2=w/(2p_2)-p_1\). Compute \(S_{12}=\partial x_1/\partial p_2+x_2\partial x_1/\partial w=1+x_2(1/(2p_1))=1+(w/(2p_2)-p_1)/(2p_1)=1+w/(4p_1p_2)-1/2=1/2+w/(4p_1p_2)\). Next \(S_{21}=\partial x_2/\partial p_1+x_1\partial x_2/\partial w=-1+x_1(1/(2p_2))=-1+(w/(2p_1)+p_2)/(2p_2)=-1+w/(4p_1p_2)+1/2=-1/2+w/(4p_1p_2)\). Since \(S_{12}-S_{21}=1\), the Slutsky matrix is not symmetric at any point in the domain. Therefore this differentiable demand system cannot be generated by a standard smooth utility maximization problem satisfying the usual integrability conditions.

title: Profit Maximization with Decreasing Returns
learning_objective: Derive factor demand, supply, and profit for a one-input decreasing returns technology.
preliminaries: A competitive firm produces output \(y=x^a\) from input \(x\ge 0\), where \(0<a<1\). Output price is \(p>0\), input price is \(w>0\), and profit is \(\pi(x)=px^a-wx\).
question: Find the profit-maximizing input demand, output supply, and profit function. Verify the second-order condition.
solution: For \(x>0\), the first-order condition is \(pa x^{a-1}=w\). Since \(a-1<0\), this gives \(x^*(p,w)=(ap/w)^{1/(1-a)}\). The second derivative is \(pa(a-1)x^{a-2}<0\), so the objective is strictly concave on \(x>0\) and the solution is unique. Output supply is \(y^*=(x^*)^a=(ap/w)^{a/(1-a)}\). Profit can be simplified using the first-order condition \(wx^*=ap(x^*)^a\). Hence \(\pi(p,w)=p(x^*)^a-wx^*=(1-a)p(x^*)^a=(1-a)p(ap/w)^{a/(1-a)}\). This is positive, homogeneous of degree one in \((p,w)\), increasing in \(p\), and decreasing in \(w\).

source.locator: profit maximization and Cobb-Douglas example around lines 1560-1786
source.notes: Inspired by local one-input profit function derivation; rewritten cleanly.

title: Constant Returns and Competitive Profits
learning_objective: Explain why constant returns technologies have zero or unbounded competitive profits.
preliminaries: A production set \(Y\) has constant returns to scale if \(y\in Y\) and \(t\ge 0\) imply \(ty\in Y\). A competitive firm facing price vector \(p\) solves \(\sup_{y\in Y} p\cdot y\).
question: Prove that if \(Y\) has constant returns to scale and there exists \(\bar y\in Y\) with \(p\cdot \bar y>0\), then no finite profit maximum exists. What happens if \(p\cdot y\le 0\) for every \(y\in Y\) and \(0\in Y\)?
solution: If \(p\cdot\bar y>0\), constant returns imply \(t\bar y\in Y\) for every \(t\ge 0\). Profits along this ray are \(p\cdot(t\bar y)=t(p\cdot\bar y)\), which diverge to infinity as \(t\to\infty\). Thus the supremum profit is infinite and no finite maximizer exists. If instead \(p\cdot y\le 0\) for all \(y\in Y\) and \(0\in Y\), the firm can choose inactivity \(0\) and earn \(0\), while no feasible plan earns more. The maximum profit is therefore \(0\), and every feasible \(y\) with \(p\cdot y=0\) is profit-maximizing. Constant returns are compatible with competitive equilibrium only at prices that do not allow strictly positive profits.

source.notes: Inspired by source discussion of CRS profit maximization and nonexistence.

title: Cost Minimization for Cobb-Douglas Production
learning_objective: Derive conditional factor demands and the cost function for a Cobb-Douglas technology.
preliminaries: A firm produces \(y=x_1^{\alpha}x_2^{1-\alpha}\), with \(0<\alpha<1\). Input prices are \(w_1,w_2>0\). The cost function is \(c(w,y)=\min_{x\ge 0} w_1x_1+w_2x_2\) subject to \(x_1^{\alpha}x_2^{1-\alpha}\ge y\).
question: Find the conditional factor demands and the cost function.
solution: At a positive target \(y>0\), the constraint binds and the solution is interior. The first-order conditions imply \(w_1/w_2=(\alpha/(1-\alpha))(x_2/x_1)\). Thus cost shares are \(w_1x_1=\alpha C\) and \(w_2x_2=(1-\alpha)C\), where \(C=c(w,y)\). Therefore \(x_1=\alpha C/w_1\) and \(x_2=(1-\alpha)C/w_2\). Substitute into production: \(y=(\alpha C/w_1)^{\alpha}((1-\alpha)C/w_2)^{1-\alpha}=C\alpha^{\alpha}(1-\alpha)^{1-\alpha}/(w_1^{\alpha}w_2^{1-\alpha})\). Hence \(c(w,y)=y w_1^{\alpha}w_2^{1-\alpha}/(\alpha^{\alpha}(1-\alpha)^{1-\alpha})\). Conditional demands are \(x_1(w,y)=\alpha c(w,y)/w_1\) and \(x_2(w,y)=(1-\alpha)c(w,y)/w_2\). For \(y=0\), both demands and cost are zero.

source.notes: Inspired by local production duality and cost-function examples.

validation.notes: Drafted with boundary at zero output; pending independent validation.

title: Recovering Net Supply from a Profit Function
learning_objective: Use Hotelling's lemma to recover net supply and check homogeneity.
preliminaries: If a profit function \(\pi(p)\) is differentiable, Hotelling's lemma says that net supply is \(y(p)=\nabla_p\pi(p)\). A valid profit function should be homogeneous of degree one and convex in prices.
question: Let \(\pi(p_1,p_2)=\sqrt{p_1^2+p_2^2}\) for \(p_1,p_2>0\). Recover net supply, and verify that \(p\cdot y(p)=\pi(p)\).
solution: Differentiate: \(y_1(p)=\partial\pi/\partial p_1=p_1/\sqrt{p_1^2+p_2^2}\) and \(y_2(p)=p_2/\sqrt{p_1^2+p_2^2}\). Then \(p\cdot y=p_1^2/\sqrt{p_1^2+p_2^2}+p_2^2/\sqrt{p_1^2+p_2^2}=\sqrt{p_1^2+p_2^2}=\pi(p)\). This equality is Euler's theorem for a differentiable homogeneous function of degree one. The function is homogeneous of degree one because \(\pi(tp)=t\pi(p)\) for \(t>0\), and it is convex because it is the Euclidean norm. Thus it has the formal properties of a profit function, and the gradient is homogeneous of degree zero.

source.locator: properties of profit function and supply from profit function around lines 2206-2514
source.notes: Inspired by source treatment of Hotelling's lemma and profit-function properties.

validation.notes: Drafted with derivative and Euler check; pending independent validation.

title: Shephard's Lemma for a CES Cost Function
learning_objective: Differentiate a cost function to obtain conditional factor demands and verify cost exhaustion.
preliminaries: Assume the cost function is \(c(w_1,w_2,y)=y(w_1^{1-\sigma}+w_2^{1-\sigma})^{1/(1-\sigma)}\), with \(\sigma>0\) and \(\sigma\ne 1\). Let \(A=w_1^{1-\sigma}+w_2^{1-\sigma}\).
question: Use Shephard's lemma to compute conditional input demands \(x_1(w,y)\) and \(x_2(w,y)\). Show that \(w_1x_1+w_2x_2=c(w,y)\).
solution: Differentiate \(c=yA^{1/(1-\sigma)}\). For input 1, \(\partial c/\partial w_1=y(1/(1-\sigma))A^{1/(1-\sigma)-1}(1-\sigma)w_1^{-\sigma}=yA^{\sigma/(1-\sigma)}w_1^{-\sigma}\). Similarly, \(x_2=yA^{\sigma/(1-\sigma)}w_2^{-\sigma}\). Cost exhaustion follows by multiplication: \(w_1x_1+w_2x_2=yA^{\sigma/(1-\sigma)}(w_1^{1-\sigma}+w_2^{1-\sigma})=yA^{\sigma/(1-\sigma)}A=yA^{1/(1-\sigma)}=c(w,y)\). The equality reflects homogeneity of degree one of the cost function in input prices.

source.notes: Inspired by local cost-function duality discussion; CES form supplied as standalone practice.

title: Two-Person Exchange Equilibrium
learning_objective: Compute a competitive equilibrium in a two-good pure exchange economy.
preliminaries: There are two consumers and two goods. Consumer \(A\) has \(u_A(x_1,x_2)=x_1^{1/2}x_2^{1/2}\) and endowment \(\omega_A=(4,0)\). Consumer \(B\) has \(u_B(x_1,x_2)=x_1^{1/3}x_2^{2/3}\) and endowment \(\omega_B=(0,6)\). Prices are positive and normalized by \(p_2=1\).
question: Find the Walrasian equilibrium price \(p_1\) and allocation.
solution: Cobb-Douglas demands spend fixed shares of wealth. Consumer \(A\)'s wealth is \(m_A=4p_1\), so \(x_{A1}=m_A/(2p_1)=2\) and \(x_{A2}=m_A/2=2p_1\). Consumer \(B\)'s wealth is \(m_B=6\), so \(x_{B1}=m_B/(3p_1)=2/p_1\) and \(x_{B2}=2m_B/3=4\). Market clearing for good 1 requires \(2+2/p_1=4\), so \(2/p_1=2\) and \(p_1=1\). Then good 2 demand is \(x_{A2}+x_{B2}=2+4=6\), so the second market clears by Walras' law and direct calculation. The equilibrium allocation is \(x_A=(2,2)\) and \(x_B=(2,4)\), with normalized prices \((1,1)\).

source.locator: general equilibrium model and exchange discussion around lines 14323-15009
source.notes: Inspired by local general equilibrium coverage; numeric economy constructed for full solution.

validation.notes: Drafted with market clearing; pending independent validation.

title: First Welfare Theorem in an Exchange Economy
learning_objective: Prove the First Welfare Theorem using budget inequalities.
preliminaries: In a pure exchange economy, an allocation \((x_i)_i\) is feasible if \(\sum_i x_i\le \sum_i \omega_i\). A Walrasian equilibrium consists of prices \(p\gg 0\) and feasible allocation \((x_i)_i\) such that each \(x_i\) maximizes preferences over \(\{x:p\cdot x\le p\cdot\omega_i\}\). Preferences are locally nonsatiated.
question: Prove that every Walrasian equilibrium allocation is Pareto efficient.
solution: Suppose an equilibrium allocation \((x_i)_i\) is not Pareto efficient. Then there is a feasible allocation \((y_i)_i\) such that every consumer weakly prefers \(y_i\) to \(x_i\), and at least one consumer strictly prefers \(y_j\) to \(x_j\). Since \(x_i\) maximizes on the budget set, any bundle strictly preferred to \(x_i\) must be unaffordable: \(p\cdot y_j>p\cdot\omega_j\). For any consumer who weakly prefers \(y_i\) to \(x_i\), local nonsatiation and optimality imply \(p\cdot y_i\ge p\cdot\omega_i\); otherwise a slightly better nearby affordable bundle would contradict optimality of \(x_i\). Summing gives \(p\cdot\sum_i y_i>p\cdot\sum_i\omega_i\). But feasibility gives \(\sum_i y_i\le\sum_i\omega_i\), and with \(p\gg0\), \(p\cdot\sum_i y_i\le p\cdot\sum_i\omega_i\), a contradiction. Thus the equilibrium allocation is Pareto efficient.

source.locator: First Welfare Theorem and revealed preference argument around lines 15006-15354
source.notes: Inspired by local welfare theorem discussion; proof stated self-contained.

validation.notes: Drafted with local nonsatiation budget proof; pending independent validation.

title: Why Convexity Matters for Supporting Prices
learning_objective: Show by example that a Pareto efficient allocation need not be supportable without convexity.
preliminaries: An allocation is supportable by prices and transfers if each consumer's assigned bundle is optimal in a budget set with those prices and assigned wealth. Convexity assumptions are central to separating preferred sets from feasible sets.
question: Consider one consumer with consumption set \(X=\{(0,0),(1,0),(0,1)\}\) and preferences \((1,0)\sim(0,1)\succ(0,0)\). The feasible set is exactly \(X\). Show that both nonzero allocations are Pareto efficient. Then show that no strictly positive price vector and wealth level can make both \((1,0)\) and \((0,1)\) uniquely optimal at the same wealth.
solution: With one consumer, an allocation is Pareto efficient if no feasible bundle is strictly preferred to it. Since \((1,0)\) and \((0,1)\) are tied for best feasible bundle, each is Pareto efficient. Suppose a strictly positive price vector \(p=(p_1,p_2)\) and wealth \(m\) make \((1,0)\) uniquely optimal. Then it must be affordable: \(p_1\le m\), and \((0,1)\), which is equally preferred, must not be affordable: \(p_2>m\). To make \((0,1)\) uniquely optimal at the same wealth requires \(p_2\le m\) and \(p_1>m\). These inequalities cannot both hold. The example illustrates the support problem caused by nonconvex feasible/preference sets: efficient points may fail to be selected by a single linear budget in the way convex welfare theorems require.

source.locator: Second Welfare Theorem and convexity index anchors around lines 18677-19861 and 28225-28266
source.notes: Inspired by local welfare theorem discussion; finite counterexample constructed for standalone clarity.

validation.notes: Drafted as a convexity warning example; pending independent validation.

title: Robinson Crusoe Production Equilibrium
learning_objective: Compute equilibrium in a one-consumer one-firm economy and connect decentralized and planner outcomes.
preliminaries: There is one consumption good \(c\) and labor \(l\). The consumer has one unit of time, leisure \(r=1-l\), and utility \(u(c,r)=\ln c+\ln r\). A firm produces \(y=\sqrt{l}\) from labor. The consumer owns the firm. Normalize the consumption good price to one, and let the wage be \(w>0\).
question: Find the competitive equilibrium wage, labor, consumption, leisure, and profit.
solution: The firm maximizes \(\sqrt{l}-wl\). The first-order condition is \(1/(2\sqrt{l})=w\), so labor demand is \(l_f=1/(4w^2)\), output is \(1/(2w)\), and profit is \(\pi=1/(2w)-w/(4w^2)=1/(4w)\). The consumer's wealth is \(w+\pi\), since selling all time would earn \(w\) and firm profit is rebated. Choosing \(c,r\) subject to \(c+wr=w+\pi\), Cobb-Douglas demands give \(r=(w+\pi)/(2w)\) and labor supply \(l_s=1-r\). Equilibrium requires \(l_s=l_f\). Substitute \(\pi=1/(4w)\): \(1-(w+1/(4w))/(2w)=1/(4w^2)\). Multiplying by \(4w^2\) gives \(4w^2-2w^2-1=1\), so \(2w^2=2\) and \(w=1\). Then \(l=1/4\), \(r=3/4\), \(y=c=1/2\), and \(\pi=1/4\).

source.notes: Inspired by local production equilibrium discussion; numeric example constructed for a full solution.

validation.notes: Drafted with firm and consumer sides solved; pending independent validation.

title: Deadweight Loss from a Unit Tax
learning_objective: Compute equilibrium incidence, revenue, and deadweight loss in a linear market.
preliminaries: A competitive market has inverse demand \(P_D(Q)=10-Q\) and inverse supply \(P_S(Q)=2+Q\). A per-unit tax \(t=2\) is imposed on sellers, so buyers pay \(P_b\), sellers receive \(P_s\), and \(P_b=P_s+t\).
question: Find the pre-tax equilibrium, post-tax equilibrium, tax revenue, and deadweight loss.
solution: Without the tax, equilibrium solves \(10-Q=2+Q\), so \(Q^0=4\) and price is \(6\). With the tax, equilibrium solves \(P_D(Q)=P_S(Q)+2\), or \(10-Q=2+Q+2\). Thus \(Q^1=3\), buyers pay \(P_b=10-3=7\), and sellers receive \(P_s=2+3=5\). Revenue is \(tQ^1=2\cdot3=6\). The quantity reduction is \(1\). The deadweight loss triangle has height \(t=2\) and base \(Q^0-Q^1=1\), so \(DWL=\frac{1}{2}\cdot2\cdot1=1\). Incidence is split equally here because demand and supply slopes have equal absolute value: buyers pay \(1\) more and sellers receive \(1\) less than the original price.

source.locator: equilibrium, welfare, and surplus themes around lines 15665-16069 and 19800-20073
source.notes: A standalone applied welfare exercise inspired by local equilibrium welfare material.

title: Affine Transformations of Expected Utility
learning_objective: Show why expected utility representations are unique only up to positive affine transformations.
preliminaries: A finite lottery \(L\) gives prize \(x_i\) with probability \(q_i\). An expected utility representation evaluates \(L\) by \(EU_u(L)=\sum_i q_i u(x_i)\).
question: Suppose \(u\) represents preferences over lotteries by expected utility. Show that \(v(x)=a u(x)+b\), with \(a>0\), represents the same preferences by expected utility. Explain why an arbitrary increasing nonlinear transform need not preserve expected utility rankings.
solution: For any lottery \(L\), \(EU_v(L)=\sum_i q_i(au(x_i)+b)=a\sum_i q_i u(x_i)+b\sum_i q_i=a EU_u(L)+b\). Therefore \(EU_v(L)\ge EU_v(M)\) if and only if \(aEU_u(L)+b\ge aEU_u(M)+b\), which holds if and only if \(EU_u(L)\ge EU_u(M)\), since \(a>0\). Thus \(v\) gives the same ranking. A nonlinear increasing transform \(f(u)\) generally fails because \(\sum_i q_i f(u(x_i))\) is not an increasing transformation of \(\sum_i q_i u(x_i)\). Expectations and nonlinear functions do not commute, so the transformed index can change attitudes toward mixtures and hence lottery rankings.

source.locator: expected utility theorem and uniqueness around lines 8020-8144
source.notes: Inspired by local proof of affine uniqueness for expected utility.

validation.notes: Drafted with affine calculation and nonlinear warning; pending independent validation.

title: Risk Premium with Log Utility
learning_objective: Compute a certainty equivalent and risk premium for a binary wealth lottery.
preliminaries: A consumer with Bernoulli utility \(u(w)=\ln w\) evaluates lotteries by expected utility. The certainty equivalent \(CE\) solves \(u(CE)=EU\). The risk premium is expected wealth minus \(CE\).
question: Initial wealth is \(100\). A fair risk adds \(+50\) or \(-50\) with equal probability. Compute expected utility, certainty equivalent, and risk premium.
solution: Final wealth is \(150\) or \(50\), each with probability \(1/2\). Expected utility is \(EU=(1/2)\ln150+(1/2)\ln50=\ln\sqrt{7500}\). The certainty equivalent solves \(\ln CE=\ln\sqrt{7500}\), so \(CE=\sqrt{7500}=50\sqrt{3}\approx86.60\). Expected wealth is \((150+50)/2=100\). Hence the risk premium is \(100-50\sqrt{3}\approx13.40\). Since \(\ln\) is strictly concave, Jensen's inequality implies \(CE<E[w]=100\), so the positive risk premium is consistent with risk aversion.

source.locator: risk aversion and expected utility around lines 8176-8236 and exercises around 9061-9073
source.notes: Inspired by local expected utility and risk aversion examples; numbers rewritten.

validation.notes: Drafted with exact CE and risk premium; pending independent validation.

title: Arrow-Pratt Approximation to the Risk Premium
learning_objective: Use local absolute risk aversion to approximate the risk premium of a small zero-mean gamble.
preliminaries: For Bernoulli utility \(u\), absolute risk aversion is \(A(w)=-u''(w)/u'(w)\). For a small zero-mean risk \(\varepsilon\) with variance \(\sigma^2\), the risk premium is approximately \(\rho\approx \frac{1}{2}A(w)\sigma^2\).
question: Let \(u(w)=\sqrt{w}\), initial wealth \(w=100\), and a zero-mean gamble \(\varepsilon\) that is \(+10\) or \(-10\) with equal probability. Compute the Arrow-Pratt approximation to the risk premium.
solution: For \(u(w)=w^{1/2}\), \(u'(w)=1/(2\sqrt{w})\) and \(u''(w)=-1/(4w^{3/2})\). Thus \(A(w)=-u''(w)/u'(w)=1/(2w)\). At \(w=100\), \(A(100)=1/200\). The gamble has mean zero and variance \(E[\varepsilon^2]=100\). The Arrow-Pratt approximation is \(\rho\approx\frac{1}{2}(1/200)(100)=1/4\). Thus the consumer would approximately give up \(0.25\) units of wealth to avoid this small risk. The approximation is local; it becomes more accurate as the gamble size shrinks relative to wealth.

source.notes: Inspired by local risk aversion measure discussion.

title: Full Insurance at an Actuarially Fair Premium
learning_objective: Show that a risk-averse expected utility maximizer fully insures at actuarially fair odds.
preliminaries: Initial wealth is \(W\). A loss \(L>0\) occurs with probability \(q\in(0,1)\). The consumer buys coverage \(I\in[0,L]\) at premium \(qI\). Bernoulli utility \(u\) is strictly increasing and strictly concave.
question: Prove that the optimal insurance choice is \(I=L\).
solution: Final wealth if no loss occurs is \(W-qI\). Final wealth if loss occurs is \(W-L+I-qI=W-L+(1-q)I\). Expected wealth is \((1-q)(W-qI)+q(W-L+(1-q)I)=W-qL\), independent of \(I\), because the premium is actuarially fair. The choice of \(I\) only changes the spread of wealth across states. Full insurance \(I=L\) gives wealth \(W-qL\) in both states. For any other \(I\), final wealth has the same mean but is nonconstant. By strict concavity, Jensen's inequality gives \(E[u(W_s)]<u(E[W_s])=u(W-qL)\) for any nonconstant wealth distribution. Therefore full insurance uniquely maximizes expected utility.

source.notes: Inspired by local insurance demand discussion; proof written from first principles.

title: Log Utility Portfolio Choice
learning_objective: Solve a simple risky-asset share problem under log utility.
preliminaries: An investor has wealth \(W>0\). A riskless asset has gross return \(1\). A risky asset has gross return \(R_H=2\) with probability \(1/2\) and \(R_L=1/2\) with probability \(1/2\). The investor puts share \(a\in[0,1]\) in the risky asset and maximizes expected \(\ln\) final wealth.
question: Find the optimal risky share \(a\).
solution: Final wealth is \(W(1+a(R-1))\). Since \(\ln W\) is constant, maximize \(f(a)=\frac{1}{2}\ln(1+a)+\frac{1}{2}\ln(1-a/2)\) over \([0,1]\). The derivative is \(f'(a)=\frac{1}{2}(1/(1+a))-\frac{1}{4}(1/(1-a/2))\). Setting \(f'(a)=0\) gives \(2/(1+a)=1/(1-a/2)\), so \(2-a=1+a\), hence \(a=1/2\). The second derivative \(f''(a)=-\frac{1}{2}(1+a)^{-2}-\frac{1}{8}(1-a/2)^{-2}<0\), so this is the unique maximizer. Boundary derivatives also confirm an interior solution: \(f'(0)=1/4>0\) and \(f'(1)<0\).

source.locator: risky asset investment and expected utility around lines 8505-8508 and exercises 9003-9034
source.notes: Inspired by local expected-utility portfolio examples; standalone binary return problem.

title: Independence Axiom and a Common Consequence Test
learning_objective: Use the independence axiom to identify an expected-utility restriction on lottery choices.
preliminaries: The independence axiom says that for lotteries \(A,B,C\) and \(\alpha\in(0,1)\), \(A\succeq B\) if and only if \(\alpha A+(1-\alpha)C\succeq \alpha B+(1-\alpha)C\).
question: Let prizes be dollars. Define \(A=(1\text{ with prob. }1)\), \(B=(5\text{ with prob. }0.1;1\text{ with prob. }0.89;0\text{ with prob. }0.01)\), \(C=(1\text{ with prob. }0.11;0\text{ with prob. }0.89)\), and \(D=(5\text{ with prob. }0.1;0\text{ with prob. }0.9)\). Show that preferences \(A\succ B\) and \(D\succ C\) violate expected utility independence.
solution: Write the first pair as mixtures with a common consequence. Let \(E\) be the sure prize \(1\), \(F\) the lottery paying \(5\) with probability \(10/11\) and \(0\) with probability \(1/11\), and \(G\) the sure prize \(0\). Then \(A=0.11E+0.89E\), while \(B=0.11F+0.89E\). Thus \(A\succ B\) implies, by independence after removing the common \(0.89E\), that \(E\succ F\). The second pair is \(C=0.11E+0.89G\) and \(D=0.11F+0.89G\). If \(E\succ F\), independence implies \(C\succ D\), not \(D\succ C\). Therefore the pattern \(A\succ B\) and \(D\succ C\) cannot be represented by expected utility preferences satisfying independence.

source.notes: Inspired by local Allais discussion; probabilities stated self-contained.

validation.notes: Drafted with common consequence decomposition; pending independent validation.

title: A Lemons Market Cutoff Equilibrium
learning_objective: Solve for market breakdown or trade in a simple adverse-selection model.
preliminaries: Car quality \(q\) is uniformly distributed on \([0,1]\). Sellers know \(q\) and value a car at \(q\). Buyers do not observe \(q\) and value a car at \(\frac{3}{2}q\). A competitive market price \(p\) is paid for every car offered. A seller sells if \(p\ge q\).
question: Find all competitive equilibrium prices with positive trade. Is there market breakdown?
solution: At price \(p\in[0,1]\), sellers with \(q\le p\) offer cars, so the conditional expected quality is \(E[q\mid q\le p]=p/2\). Buyers' expected value of an offered car is \((3/2)(p/2)=3p/4\). In a competitive equilibrium with positive trade and risk-neutral buyers, price must equal expected buyer value, so \(p=3p/4\), implying \(p=0\). This contradicts positive trade with \(p>0\). If \(p>1\), all cars are offered and buyers' expected value is \((3/2)(1/2)=3/4\), so such a price cannot be sustained. Thus the only equilibrium is market breakdown at \(p=0\), with no positive-quality sellers willing to sell. The adverse selection feedback lowers expected quality below any positive candidate price.

source.locator: lemons market and adverse selection around lines 21144-21197
source.notes: Inspired by local lemons-market discussion; continuous-quality version written standalone.

validation.notes: Drafted with cutoff and no-trade proof; pending independent validation.

title: When a Lemons Market Has Positive Trade
learning_objective: Show how stronger buyer surplus can restore positive trade in a lemons market.
preliminaries: Quality \(q\sim U[0,1]\). Sellers value a car at \(q\). Buyers value it at \(kq\), where \(k>1\). At price \(p\), sellers with \(q\le p\) sell.
question: Assume \(k=3\). Find competitive equilibrium prices and traded qualities. Compare with the case \(k=3/2\).
solution: For \(p\in[0,1]\), the offered qualities are \([0,p]\), with conditional mean \(p/2\). Buyers' expected value is \(k p/2=3p/2\), which exceeds \(p\) for every \(p>0\). Competition among buyers therefore pushes price up; no interior cutoff price can satisfy zero expected profits. If \(p=1\), all sellers offer, mean quality is \(1/2\), and buyer value is \(3/2\), above the price. A competitive price cannot exceed buyer value, but any price in \([1,3/2]\) attracts all cars and gives buyers nonnegative surplus, while price competition with free entry selects \(p=3/2\) if buyers bid expected value. Thus full trade is sustainable when buyer value is high enough. For \(k=3/2\), the cutoff equation gave \(p=3p/4\), so only no trade survived. The assumption change reverses the adverse-selection conclusion.

title: Separating Insurance Contracts with Two Risk Types
learning_objective: Check incentive compatibility and zero profit for a simple menu of insurance contracts.
preliminaries: There are two risk types. A type \(H\) has loss probability \(q_H=1/2\); type \(L\) has loss probability \(q_L=1/4\). Loss size is \(D=100\). Initial wealth is \(200\). A contract \((P,I)\) charges premium \(P\) and pays indemnity \(I\) if loss occurs. Insurers are risk neutral and break even type by type if \(P=q_t I\). Consumers have expected utility with \(u(w)=\sqrt{w}\).
question: Consider contracts \(C_H=(50,100)\) and \(C_L=(10,40)\). Do they break even type by type? Does the menu separate the two types?
solution: Break-even premiums are \(q_HI_H=(1/2)100=50\) and \(q_LI_L=(1/4)40=10\), so each contract breaks even for its intended type. Compute expected utilities. Type \(H\) choosing \(C_H\) gets wealth \(150\) in both states, so utility \(\sqrt{150}\). Type \(H\) choosing \(C_L\) gets no-loss wealth \(190\) and loss wealth \(130\), so expected utility \((1/2)\sqrt{190}+(1/2)\sqrt{130}\), which is approximately \(12.59\), above \(\sqrt{150}\approx12.25\). Thus \(H\) would deviate to \(C_L\). The menu does not separate, even though each contract breaks even for its intended type. The low-risk contract gives too much coverage at too low a premium from the high-risk type's perspective.

title: Moral Hazard and a Bonus Contract
learning_objective: Derive the minimum bonus that implements high effort in a binary-output agency problem.
preliminaries: An agent chooses effort \(e\in\{L,H\}\). High output \(Y=100\) occurs with probability \(0.8\) under \(H\) and \(0.4\) under \(L\). Low output is \(0\). Effort costs are \(c_H=10\), \(c_L=0\). The agent is risk neutral, has outside option \(0\), and wages must be nonnegative. A contract pays \(b\) after high output and \(s\) after low output.
question: Find the cheapest contract that induces high effort and satisfies participation. What is the principal's expected profit under that contract?
solution: The agent's expected utility from high effort is \(0.8b+0.2s-10\). From low effort it is \(0.4b+0.6s\). Incentive compatibility for high effort requires \(0.8b+0.2s-10\ge0.4b+0.6s\), or \(0.4(b-s)\ge10\), so \(b-s\ge25\). Participation requires \(0.8b+0.2s-10\ge0\). To minimize expected wage cost under high effort, set the low-output wage as low as possible: \(s=0\). Then incentive compatibility requires \(b\ge25\), and participation is \(0.8b-10\ge0\), requiring \(b\ge12.5\). Thus \(b=25,s=0\) is cheapest. Expected wage cost is \(0.8\cdot25=20\). Expected output under high effort is \(80\), so the principal's expected profit is \(80-20=60\).

title: A Separating Education Signaling Equilibrium
learning_objective: Verify separating equilibrium conditions in a two-type signaling model.
preliminaries: Workers are high productivity \(H\) or low productivity \(L\). Productivities are \(y_H=10\) and \(y_L=4\). A worker chooses education \(e\ge0\), which does not affect productivity. Education cost is \(c_H(e)=e\) for \(H\) and \(c_L(e)=2e\) for \(L\). Competitive firms pay expected productivity conditional on observed education.
question: Find values of \(e^*>0\) for which there is a separating equilibrium where \(H\) chooses \(e^*\), \(L\) chooses \(0\), firms pay \(10\) after \(e^*\), and firms pay \(4\) after \(0\).
solution: Given the proposed beliefs, type \(H\) gets payoff \(10-e^*\) from choosing \(e^*\) and payoff \(4\) from mimicking \(L\) at \(0\). Thus \(H\)'s incentive constraint is \(10-e^*\ge4\), or \(e^*\le6\). Type \(L\) gets payoff \(4\) from choosing \(0\) and payoff \(10-2e^*\) from mimicking \(H\). Thus \(L\)'s incentive constraint is \(4\ge10-2e^*\), or \(e^*\ge3\). Therefore any \(e^*\in[3,6]\) can support the separating allocation with the stated on-path beliefs and competitive wages. Off-path beliefs can assign any unexpected education level to type \(L\), inducing wage \(4\), which prevents profitable deviations to other education levels. The least-cost separating signal is \(e^*=3\).
